title: RA.982 Barker

translation.content: Sam Barker asks more from techno. The artist, known simply as Barker, is one of electronic music's most consistently conscientious and curious producers, challenging listeners to question the norms we accept about our shared culture—whether it's the music that fills the room, the process behind it or the purpose of the space itself. British-born yet based in Berlin since 2007, Barker forged a connection to many of the city's leading institutions, including Ostgut Ton, and over the course of a long and fruitful relationship, he carved out one of the more singular paths on the club and label's roster. Not one for orthodoxy, Barker challenged the four-to-the-floor techno framework in favour of melodic experimentation. By decentering or completely stripping away the typical trappings of kick drums and claps, releases such as Debiasing and debut album Utility showcased his ability to craft music that's both light and immersive, buoyant in low-end presence and shimmering in weightless space. As Philip Sherburne wrote of Utility, "Barker [borrows] ambient's billowing forms and reinforces them for the needs of the dance floor." Six years after Utility, his second album, Stochastic Drift, arrives on Smalltown Supersound this month. Shaped by pandemic-driven reinvention, it burrows deeper into harmonic twists and freeform drift. "At some point I became conscious of the process," he wrote of his latest album. "The only thing you can do is embrace the uncertainty and see every change, as a potential positive." Consider his RA Podcast another sequel. Like his much-beloved 2019 mix for FACT, it's a collage of live recordings and a fitting expression of the artist's own internal spring. RA.982 radiates wide-eyed optimism: percussion cloaked in foggy, swirling pads and trance-like chords, neatly synched in synthetic glimmers. All in all, it's an hour of music crafted for contemplation, collective euphoria or heads-down epiphany—or for that matter, any moment, really, its emotive depth seemingly endless. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I've just finished recording an album with saxophonist Bendik Giske, which has been really exciting. Otherwise preparing myself for a few improvised events in the coming months, developing my solo live show, various interviews and press stuff, updating my studio cabling and general workflow improvements—and I just came back from the dentist. This Podcast, uncommonly, is a blend of original live recordings—why that approach? I wanted to share what I've been doing in parallel to the studio productions these past few years, similar to the FACT mix I did around Utility. Playing live is kind of like my sketch pad, where I can test ideas out with an audience and see what works. Also I wanted to do something other than a DJ mix for RA, as it's a special series. Your new album, Stochastic Drift, arrives this week six years after Utility. How has your production style evolved in that time? I've been getting much deeper into mechanical acoustic instruments, which has opened up a new world of sound and new techniques in recording. I've spent less money on synths and more on microphones and preamps. It's been a fun challenge. In terms of approach, the most significant change has been my focus moving away from the dance floor somewhat. Having more opportunities for concert type shows over these years was a chance to explore a wider range of intentions than just dancing. The absence of kick drums can be found across your work. What drew you to explore rhythm in this way? On the one hand, it's a way to strip a track of its primary genre signifier, reducing it simply to music that can be used for a broader range of purposes. I like the challenge of making music that can be multi-functional, and pushing that quality as far as possible. Other than that, I became tired of the pushy attitude you get with a constant four-to-the-floor thump dictating how you should behave. You've mentioned that your perfectionism often led to "terabytes of failed prototypes." So how did you know Stochastic Drift was done and ready to go? I tend to defer this decision to the label or A&R, as there is a process of detachment that grows the longer you work on something. This record was hard because it spans a long period of time and a few different approaches, so getting everything to work together was a challenge. Having good feedback from fresh ears is essential for me. Along with Joakim at Smalltown, I had the help of Alex Samuels, who was running Ostgut Ton, and was a part of this process for Debiasing and Utility. With the new label, it was handy to have someone on board that was familiar with my process and my idiosyncrasies. You were heavily involved with Leisure System and Ostgut Ton throughout the 2010s. How has your experience in Berlin's music scene in the 2020s so far been? In the clubs, aside from a noticeable increase in BPM, I think there is a growing audience for sounds beyond the techno/house binary. At least, less purist than when I arrived here in 2007. There's also a handful of new listening bars that are loosely modelled on the kind of spaces you find in Tokyo back alleys, with luxurious audiophile sound systems and all attention to the music. KWIA, after, migas and Bar Niero all opened up in the past few years and are a refreshing addition to the Berlin nightlife. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Big question, but in short I feel like it's the quality of our attention that matters most right now. I worry that the level of exposure to all the terrible things happening in the world is making us numb. It seems most people are already watching closely in horror as the genocide in Gaza continues, Lebanon, Sudan, the war in Ukraine, which is important, but we should take care not to become desensitised by non-stop exposure to violence and cruelty. Something that worries me about the information environments we commonly inhabit: I feel like we are dangerously unaware of how propaganda works in the age of social media. When images of bomb-shredded children are interspersed with banalities, memes, DJs, products, hot girls, funny fails and so on, it has the effect of trivialising the horrors, while scattering our attention to the point of incapacitation. We feel we're keeping ourselves informed but there is no depth in this way of consuming information, it all ultimately gets filed under trivia. Peter Pomerantsev described this recent propaganda technique like this: "instead of simply oppressing opposition, as had been the case with 20th-century strains, it climbs inside all ideologies and movements, exploiting and rendering them absurd." This quote sticks with me, and I notice the technique in action all the time. We need to get a tighter grip on our minds and our social movements, and be on the lookout for malevolent forces, trying to manoeuvre our outrage towards counterproductive action, misguided slogans or just plain apathy. We should at least try to reclaim our attention from the toxic slop feed of social media. It's a giant unregulated psychology experiment full of bad actors, bots and misinformation, and I try to keep it at a distance to maintain some degree of mental hygiene. I don't have a perfect answer for an alternative, but whatever the subject you're interested in, it's better to read books, watch documentaries, listen to podcasts and sign up for newsletters from reputable writers. It doesn't hurt to read about how the mind works, too. We need to take back control of what we pay attention to, and what helps me is to allot time to subjects I care about, and give them my full attention. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I could give a lot of examples, but the first that came to mind is a festival in Taiwan called Organik, run by Josh who is also behind the club Pawnshop. The location moved now, but it used to take place in Hualien, on a beach surrounded by mountains and jungle, with everybody camping on the beach. Beautiful location aside, the programming over two days is just impeccable, perfectly matching the phases of the party, from daytime ambience to peak time, winding down for sunrise. It always feels like the right sound at the right moment. I played several years in a row before the pandemic hit, and Taipei became a cosy home base for touring in Asia. There's a great local scene in Taiwan, I made a lot of good friends and I would say it has influenced my productions too. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm doing an album launch in London at Jazz Cafe on April 16th, and otherwise I have a few collaborations I'm excited for in the coming months: there's the project I mentioned with Bendik Giske; I'm also playing two shows in Switzerland at the start of May with an amazing drummer called Arthur Hnatek. April 5th is an eight-hour live jam with Stoor x Circle of Live in Amsterdam, alongside Speedy J, Frank Wiedemann, Sebastian Muellert and JakoJako. I'm also playing a live set with JakoJako in Berghain next month, downstairs in Säule. After some years of solo work, it makes me very happy to perform with other people again
